original_abstract: While alkyl ammonium lead halide perovskites have emerged as highly efficient materials for thin-film photovoltaics, their long-term stability under operational conditions remains a significant challenge. This study investigates the origin of photo-induced degradation in planar heterostructure solar cells using methylammonium (MA), formamidinium (FA), and mixed-cation (MAxFA1-x) lead triiodide active layers. We demonstrate that the efficiency loss observed under continuous solar illumination is quasi-reversible and strongly linked to light-induced, thermally activated ion transport. Using in operando electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS), we identified a low-frequency Warburg diffusion regime that appears specifically under illumination, allowing for the quantification of ionic diffusion coefficients and activation energies. Our results indicate that MA+ ions migrate more rapidly than the larger FA+ ions, and that MAPbI3 exhibits a sharp decrease in activation energy above 320 K, coinciding with a tetragonal-to-cubic phase transition and significant lattice expansion observed via powder X-ray diffraction (PXRD). In contrast, mixed-cation MAxFA1-xPbI3 films exhibit higher activation barriers for ion transport and superior structural stability, resulting in suppressed degradation. Furthermore, we show that performance decay is primarily accelerated by the infrared (IR) component of the solar spectrum, which facilitates ion migration through heating. By implementing IR-cut filters or active cooling, we significantly extended the operational lifetime of these devices. These findings suggest that managing ion transport kinetics through cation engineering and thermal regulation is critical for the development of stable perovskite solar cells.
humanized_abstract: In recent years, alkyl ammonium lead halide perovskites have been extensively studied as the active material in thin film photovoltaic devices due to their remarkable power conversion efficiency. However, their long term stability under operational conditions remains unresolved. In this research, photo-induced degradation of planar heterostructure solar cells using different active materials such as methylammonium (MA) lead triiodide, formamidinium (FA) lead triiodide and mixed-cation (MA Fairfaxa1-x) lead triiodide is investigated. Herein, we demonstrate that quasi-reversible loss of efficiency under continuous solar illumination, which is attributed to a large extent attributed to light-induced, thermally activated ion movement. Using in operando electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS), we have successfully probed the photo-induced ionic movement in different perovskite materials and derived their ionic diffusion coefficients and activation energies, respectively. The mobilites of different cations were derived which revealed that the smaller MA+ ions moved faster than the bigger FA+ ions. Moreover, MAPbI3 has a sharp reduction in the activation energy above 320 K which coincides with the onset of the tetragonal-to-cubic phase transition and its large lattice expansion as observed with powder X-ray diffraction (PXRD). Furthermore, the mixed-cation MA Fairfaxa1-xPbI3 films showed higher activation barriers of ion movement and maintained their structural integrity which prevented the degradation of solar cells. In addition, the dominant role of infrared component of the solar spectrum in accelerating the ion movement and the performance degradation is elucidated. Utilizing an IR-cut filter or cooling the solar cells extends their operational lifetime. This research gives a comprehensive understanding into the ion movement in perovskite solar cells and indicates that controlling the ion movement by engineering cations and cooling the solar cells are important towards realising a stable solar cell device.
